questions.question: What does VLAN stand for?
options: Virtual Local Area Network
Variable Link Access Node
Verified LAN Architecture
Virtual Layer Access Network

questions.question: Why is a router or Layer 3 switch required when using VLANs?
options: To assign IP addresses to every port
To connect VLANs and mediate between different IP subnets
To encrypt traffic between switch ports
To replace managed switches entirely

questions.question: What is the main security benefit of VLAN segmentation?
options: It eliminates the need for firewalls
It makes access to data and devices in different subnets harder for attackers
It prevents all broadcasts on the network
It automatically encrypts all Ethernet frames

questions.question: How does VLAN segmentation reduce network load?
options: By disabling QoS on all switches
By keeping broadcasts within their respective subnet
By removing the need for routers
By limiting each VLAN to a single device

questions.question: In a port-based VLAN, how many VLANs are assigned to each switch port?
options: Exactly one
Up to four
As many as the administrator chooses per packet
None; ports are shared dynamically

questions.question: How long is the VLAN tag in a tagged VLAN Ethernet frame?
options: 2 bytes
4 bytes
8 bytes
12 bytes

questions.question: How many VLANs can be created using the 12-bit VLAN ID in the tag?
What characterizes a static VLAN?
options: Port assignment changes automatically based on packet content
Port assignment is permanently specified until reconfigured by the administrator
VLANs are created only through tagging on every port
Devices must move physically to change VLAN membership

questions.question: How does a dynamic VLAN assign a port to a VLAN?
options: The administrator manually reprograms the port for every device
The switch uses programmable rules based on packet characteristics such as MAC address or protocol type
Each port is permanently bound to one VLAN at factory setup
Tagged frames are stripped before the switch makes any decision

questions.question: In modern switches, what is typically true of access ports versus trunk ports?
options: Access ports use tagging; trunk ports are port-based only
Access ports connect devices to one VLAN; trunk ports carry tagged traffic between network devices
Both require every connected device to handle VLAN tags
Trunk ports are used only for end-user workstations
